summary:
Deep tendon reflexes are usually preserved in patients with myasthenia gravis and can be used as an indicator when differentiating from other neurological diseases characterized by altered reflex responses due to involvement at various levels along the neural pathway. Myasthenia gravis does not involve loss of deep tendon reflexes under normal circumstances without additional complications. To answer "all" options correctly implies one must identify what doesn't fit within MG characteristics; hence C stands out based on common presentation patterns described here. ```plaintext
Deep tendon reflexes are generally well-preserved in myasthenia gravis, making their absence indicative of another condition. Therefore, all listed symptoms should align with maintaining intact reflexes unless otherwise specified.
```
